questions.question: At the synapse, the conversion of the signal occurs in which of the following manners?
questions.explanation: At the nerve ending, the electrical impulse sets off the release of some chemicals (neurotransmitters). These chemicals cross the gap (synapse).

questions.question: Which of the following describes the mechanism of the 'sensitive plant' (Mimosa pudica) moving its leaves without the use of specialized muscle proteins?

options.A: Contractile proteins similar to animal muscles
options.B: Changing the amount of water in cells, resulting in swelling or shrinking
options.C: Rapid cell division on the underside of the leaf
options.D: Nervous tissue conducting impulses to the petiole

questions.explanation: Plant cells change shape by changing the amount of water in them, resulting in swelling or shrinking, and therefore in changing shapes.

questions.question: The 'Hypothalamus' plays a crucial role in the endocrine system by:

options.A: Secreting Insulin directly into the blood
options.B: Releasing hormones that regulate the secretion of pituitary hormones
options.D: Controlling the reflex arcs of the spinal cord

questions.explanation: The hypothalamus produces releasing hormones which stimulate the pituitary gland to release its hormones.

questions.question: When a tendril comes in contact with a support, it circles it. This is because:

options.A: Auxin promotes rapid growth in the part of the tendril in contact with the object
options.B: The part of the tendril in contact with the object does not grow as rapidly as the part away from the object
options.C: Contact stimulates the production of Abscisic acid
options.D: The support provides nutrients for faster growth

questions.explanation: The part of the tendril away from the object grows more rapidly than the part in contact with the object, causing the tendril to circle the object.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the 'Blood-Brain Barrier' protection mechanism is implicitly supported by the text's description of brain protection?

options.A: The brain is protected by the vertebral column
options.B: The brain sits in a fluid-filled balloon which acts as a shock absorber
options.C: The brain is directly attached to the skull to prevent movement
options.D: Brain tissue is replaced every 7 years to maintain coordination

questions.explanation: The brain is contained in a bony box. Inside the box, the brain is contained in a fluid-filled balloon which provides further shock absorption.

questions.question: In a synapse, the 'Post-synaptic' neuron receives the signal via receptors located on its:
questions.explanation: Chemicals cross the synapse and start a similar electrical impulse in a dendrite of the next neuron.

questions.question: Identify the endocrine gland that is present in females but absent in males:
questions.explanation: Ovaries are female-specific endocrine glands that produce estrogen and progesterone.

questions.question: A person is struggling with the regulation of their 'Breathing rate' and 'Blood pressure'. Damage to which specific part of the brain is most likely?
questions.explanation: All involuntary actions including blood pressure, salivation and vomiting are controlled by the medulla in the hindbrain.

questions.question: What happens to the level of 'Insulin' in the blood if the sugar levels fall?
questions.explanation: The timing and amount of hormone released are regulated by feedback mechanisms. If sugar levels fall, insulin secretion is reduced.

questions.question: Which plant hormone is technically considered a 'Growth Inhibitor' and is responsible for the wilting of leaves?
questions.explanation: Abscisic acid is one example of a hormone which inhibits growth. Its effects include wilting of leaves.

questions.question: The 'Peripheral Nervous System' (PNS) facilitates communication between the CNS and the rest of the body. It consists of:
questions.explanation: The PNS consists of cranial nerves arising from the brain and spinal nerves arising from the spinal cord.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of 'Negative Geotropism'?

options.B: Growth of shoots upwards away from the earth
options.D: Growth of pollen tubes towards the ovule

questions.explanation: Roots are positively geotropic (towards gravity) while shoots are negatively geotropic (away from gravity).

questions.question: During a reflex action, the signal is processed in the spinal cord because:

options.A: The brain is not involved in any reflex action
options.B: The thinking process of the brain is too slow for immediate danger
options.C: The spinal cord has more neurons than the brain
options.D: Muscles are directly connected to the spinal cord only

questions.explanation: The thinking process of the brain is not fast enough. Reflex arcs evolved in order to allow quick responses.

questions.question: Cytokinins are naturally found in high concentrations in:

options.B: Areas of rapid cell division like fruits and seeds
options.C: The xylem vessels of the stem
options.D: The shady side of the shoot

questions.explanation: Cytokinins promote cell division, and it is natural then that they are present in greater concentrations in areas of rapid cell division.

questions.question: The feeling of 'fullness' (satiety) after eating is detected by a center located in the:
questions.explanation: There are separate areas of the forebrain specialized for hearing, smell, sight and so on, including a separate center associated with hunger.

questions.question: The 'Master Gland' (Pituitary) is itself under the control of:
questions.explanation: The hypothalamus plays an important role in the release of many hormones, including the releasing hormones for the pituitary.

questions.question: Phototropism in shoots is caused by the 'unequal distribution' of:
questions.explanation: When light comes from one side, auxin diffuses towards the shady side of the shoot, stimulating cells to grow longer there.

questions.question: A student accidentally touches a hot object. The 'Effector' in the resulting reflex arc would be:
questions.explanation: The effector is the part of the body (usually a muscle or gland) that carries out the response.

questions.question: Iodine is essential for the thyroid gland to make thyroxin. Thyroxin regulates the metabolism of:
questions.explanation: Thyroxin regulates carbohydrate, protein and fat metabolism in the body so as to provide the best balance for growth.

questions.question: Which part of the brain is the 'Pons' located in?
questions.explanation: The hindbrain consists of the pons, medulla, and cerebellum.

questions.question: Chemotropism is best illustrated by:

options.A: Roots growing towards a water source
options.B: Bending of the plant towards sunlight
options.C: Growth of pollen tubes towards ovules

questions.explanation: Growth of pollen tubes towards ovules is an example of chemotropism (response to chemical stimulus).

questions.question: The 'Adrenaline' hormone is secreted directly into the:
questions.explanation: Adrenaline is an endocrine hormone; all endocrine hormones are secreted directly into the blood.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between 'Nervous' and 'Hormonal' control systems as per the chapter?

options.A: Nervous system is slower but reaches every cell
options.B: Hormonal system is faster and reaches every cell
options.C: Nervous system is fast but cannot reach every cell; Hormones reach all cells but are slower
options.D: Nervous system uses chemicals while hormones use electricity

questions.explanation: Electrical impulses reach only cells connected by nervous tissue. Chemical signaling (hormones) can reach all cells of the body via blood.

questions.question: The 'Reflex Arc' connects which two types of neurons directly or through a relay neuron?
questions.explanation: A reflex arc is a pathway that connects a sensory neuron to a motor neuron.

questions.question: A deficiency of 'Growth Hormone' in childhood leads to:
questions.explanation: Growth hormone is one of the hormones secreted by the pituitary. If there is a deficiency of this hormone in childhood, it designs dwarfism.
